events.event_description: Diplomatic relations established and the American Legation in Belgrade opened when U.S. Consul General Eugene Schuyler presented his credentials to the Serbian government, though Schuyler was resident at Athens.

evidence.quote: Diplomatic relations between the U.S. and Serbia were established on November 10, 1882, when U.S. Consul General Eugene Schuyler presented his credentials to the government of Serbia. At this time, the American Legation in Belgrade was established, though Schuyler was resident at Athens.

events.event_description: The American Legation moved to London following the Yugoslav government going into exile in England. Anthony J. Drexel Biddle Jr. presented credentials as U.S. Minister Near the Government of Yugoslavia in England, resident in London.

evidence.quote: Anthony J. Drexel Biddle Jr. presented his credentials as the U.S. Minister Near the Government of Yugoslavia in England on October 3, 1941, and was resident in London.

events.event_description: The American Legation Belgrade was formally raised to Embassy status with the appointment of Anthony J. Drexel Biddle Jr. as Ambassador Extraordinary and Plenipotentiary.

evidence.quote: The American Legation Belgrade was raised to the status of Embassy with the appointment of Anthony J. Drexel Biddle Jr. as Ambassador Extraordinary and Plenipotentiary on September 29, 1942.

events.event_description: The U.S. Embassy followed the Yugoslav government-in-exile to Egypt. Lincoln MacVeagh presented credentials as U.S. Ambassador Near the Government of Yugoslavia Established in Egypt.

evidence.quote: On September 28, 1943, the Government of Yugoslavia was transferred to Egypt. Lincoln MacVeagh, the U.S. Ambassador Near the Government of Yugoslavia Established in Egypt, presented his credentials on December 9, 1943.

events.event_description: The U.S. Embassy followed the Yugoslav government-in-exile back to England. Rudolf E. Schoenfeld was designated U.S. Chargé d'Affaires ad interim near the Government of Yugoslavia Established in England, later succeeded by Ambassador Patterson.

evidence.quote: On July 1, 1944, Rudolf E. Schoenfeld was designated U.S. Chargé d'Affaires ad interim near the Government of Yugoslavia Established in England. On November 17, 1944, Richard Cunningham Patterson presented his credentials as U.S. Ambassador near the Government of Yugoslavia Established in England.

events.event_description: Embassy Belgrade returned to Yugoslavia and resumed operations in Belgrade as the Yugoslav government returned from exile near the end of the war.

evidence.quote: On March 31, 1945, Embassy Belgrade returned to Yugoslavia and resumed operations in Belgrade. Richard Cunningham Patterson continued to serve as U.S. Ambassador to Yugoslavia and was resident in Belgrade.

events.event_description: The U.S. announced it would not recognize the Federal Republic of Yugoslavia as a successor state to the SFRY. Ambassador Zimmerman was recalled, and the U.S. took the position that the SFRY had ceased to exist, effectively terminating diplomatic relations.

evidence.quote: On May 21, 1992, the U.S. announced that it would not recognize the Federal Republic of Yugoslavia (FRY) as a successor state of the Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia (SFRY). The FRY was composed of the Republics of Serbia and Montenegro. U.S. Ambassador Warren Zimmerman was recalled from Belgrade, but the mission continued with a staff under the authority of a Chargé d'Affaires ad interim.
